content:
AI is an extraordinary assistant, but we should be cautious when we use it to replace human judgment in situations where empathy, intuition, and human potential matter.
AI has undeniably become a productivity factor in our personal and professional lives. I personally love AI Assistants for their contribution towards my professional and personal growth. Besides the facilitation of day-to-day tasks and providing the information I need to execute certain things; it has become my educational buddy. Through AI, I am enriching my professional understanding of my functional area of expertise, Marketing and Corporate Communication. I am also expanding on my general knowledge.
The other day I was meeting someone from the Congo. AI helped me understand some key points about their culture and even recommended a place I can try one of the Congo‘’s favorite dishes, Moambe Chicken! This honestly led to a more pleasant conversation during my meeting, where my cultural awareness allowed me to relate to their culture, enriching the engagement and strengthening the trust factor.
To put it simply, I am an advocate and lover of AI Assistants. It’s been 2 years since I adopted AI Assistants such as ChatGPT, and my attachment grows daily. There is not a day that passes by without me entering and engaging with at least 10–20 prompts and their follow-up recommendations. However, the purpose of this article is to discuss how the glowing optimism I had for AI is starting to dim slightly, just like when the sun slowly transitions from the zenith to sunset, becoming less radiant and colder.
AI is an extraordinary technological achievement built on massive datasets and sophisticated algorithms. Its ability to analyze information and generate insights can appear almost limitless. However, beneath that impressive capability is software — not consciousness, emotion, intuition, or human connection.
AI can construct an amazing course for me to learn Mandarin, but it can never relay how beautiful it feels to start communicating with an individual from China. AI can teach me how to play the drums, but it will never pinpoint the exact strengths and weaknesses of my playing style, like a motivating and encouraging drum instructor would. Its usefulness remains limited by the boundaries of what technology can understand versus what humans can experience. It will most definitely continue to play an important role in our lives, but it has to be purposed very carefully.
The mere mis purposing of AI has tainted its image slightly, based on my real-world experiences with the technology so far. This article aims to shine the light on the use of AI from a business perspective, specifically the recruitment process that numerous companies are starting to re-invent based on AI adoption. Keep in mind that some of these companies are multi-national large organizations with a long history of operations and positive reputation.
My career spanned across 18 years so far and about 5 years ago, I was recruiting for a manager position in my department. Human resources utilized the AI programs of the time to shortlist a number of CVs and scheduled interview slots with the candidates I selected. One of the candidates started off the interview weak and un-settled, beyond the normal discomfort of starting an interview. She was extremely nervous. I could see it in her face and hear it in her voice. My initial impression was that this is going to be a quick video call, but I would give her the curtesy of not immediately terminating the interview.
After a bit of encouragement and trying to speak to her in a re-assuring style, I asked her specific questions in our technical area of expertise. I really listened to her replies and tried to create a conversation that was fluid, but initially simple to help her get comfortable. It would be an understatement to say that she shocked me. An interview scheduled for an hour, that I was planning to terminate in the first 10–15 minutes, turned into a 120-minute conversation with a star of a candidate. I listened and supported all while she grew in confidence. We hired her and she was star performer on the team for 4 years.
My concern isn’t AI itself. My concern is what happens when companies begin outsourcing human judgment to algorithms — especially during recruitment, where careers and lives can be changed by a single automated decision. Companies are currently scheduling interviews with short-listed candidates with AI! There is no current version of AI that can fully replicate the holistic judgment of an experienced human interviewer — someone who can recognize potential, emotional cues, resilience, and hidden strengths beyond a resume. Even if this is just a filtration step towards a human interviewer down the process, I honestly do not want to speak to a robot and I end up rejecting these interview invitations with AI !
The real question is not whether AI will continue to evolve! It is whether organizations will remain wise enough to understand that human judgment in certain processes is irreplaceable and absolutely necessary to achieve the desired outcomes.
My next article will explore other situations where careless AI adoption may create unintended consequences for organizations rather than the improvements they hoped to achieve.
Would love to hear your thoughts about AI in your workplace! Consider leaving a reaction or comment.
